stem: Let \( f \) be continuous on \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \) with \( [[m]] \le f(x) \le [[M]] \) for every \( x \) in \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \). Without computing any particular integral, what is the greatest possible value of \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f(x)\, dx \) consistent with these bounds?
solution: Since \( f(x) \le [[M]] \) on an interval of length \( [[b]] - ([[a]]) \), the comparison property gives \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f(x)\,dx \le [[M]]\,([[b]] - ([[a]])) = [[answer]] \), with equality when \( f \equiv [[M]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the least possible value, using the lower bound \( [[m]] \) instead of the upper bound.
This uses the midpoint of the bounds rather than the maximum, giving an average-based estimate.
This multiplies the range of \( f \) by the width, which measures spread, not the maximum integral.
This multiplies the maximum by the sum of the endpoints instead of the length of the interval.
This mismatches which endpoint pairs with which bound.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: On \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \), functions \( f \) and \( g \) satisfy \( g(x) - f(x) = (x - [[r]])^{2} + [[k]] \) for every \( x \), which is never negative. By the comparison property for integrals, by exactly how much must \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} g(x)\, dx \) exceed \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f(x)\, dx \)?
solution: Since \( g(x) - f(x) = (x-[[r]])^{2} + [[k]] \ge 0 \) on \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \), the comparison property gives \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} g\,dx - \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f\,dx = \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} \left[(x-[[r]])^{2} + [[k]]\right] dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the squared term entirely and only integrates the constant \( [[k]] \).
This misapplies the power rule to \( (x-[[r]])^{2} \) as if its antiderivative had exponent 2 (dividing by 2) instead of 3.
This drops the constant term \( [[k]] \) from the antiderivative.
This replaces the squared term by the cube of the interval width, ignoring \( [[r]] \) entirely.
This is off by the width of the interval.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = x - [[c]] \) on \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \); since \( [[c]] \) lies strictly between \( [[a]] \) and \( [[b]] \), \( f \) changes sign there. By how much does \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} |f(x)|\, dx \) exceed \( \left| \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f(x)\, dx \right| \)? (This shows the triangle inequality \( \left|\int f\right| \le \int |f| \) is generally strict when \( f \) changes sign, unlike the bound obtained without comparing signs at all.)
solution: Compute both sides directly: \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} |x-[[c]]|\,dx \) sums the two triangular pieces on either side of the corner at \( x=[[c]] \), while \( \left|\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} (x-[[c]])\,dx\right| \) is the absolute value of the signed net area. The gap between them is \( [[answer]] \), confirming the inequality is strict here since \( f \) changes sign.
distractor_rationales: This is \( \int |f| \) alone, forgetting to subtract \( \left|\int f\right| \).
This is \( \left|\int f\right| \) alone, forgetting to subtract it from \( \int |f| \).
This assumes the triangle inequality is always an equality, which fails whenever \( f \) changes sign.
This drops the absolute value on the integrand altogether.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The function \( f(x) = x^{3} + [[m]]x + [[k]] \) is increasing on \( [[[a]], [[b]]] \), since \( f'(x) = 3x^{2} + [[m]] > 0 \) everywhere. Because \( f \) is increasing, the left-endpoint rectangle estimate \( f([[a]])\cdot([[b]]-[[a]]) \) underestimates \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f(x)\, dx \). By exactly how much does the true integral exceed this estimate, without needing the estimate to compute the true value?
solution: The true value is \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f(x)\,dx \); the left-endpoint rectangle estimate is \( f([[a]])([[b]]-[[a]]) \). Subtracting the estimate from the true value gives the guaranteed positive gap \( [[answer]] \), since an increasing function's graph lies above its left rectangle.
distractor_rationales: This is the true integral alone, forgetting to subtract the rectangle estimate.
This uses the right-endpoint (overestimate) rectangle and reverses the subtraction.
This is the right-endpoint rectangle estimate itself, not the gap to the true value.
This is the left-endpoint rectangle estimate itself, not the gap to the true value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
